coredata.dc:title: A novel determination method for Ag(I) in environmental samples based on reduction of absorbance and fluorescence quenching of Eosin Y
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jian
Chen, Ling
Li, Yanan
Shen, Wei
Manley-Harris, Merilyn
coredata.dc:description: Ag(I) and Eosin Y (EY) can interact directly under weakly acidic conditions to form a sparingly soluble ionic association which results in absorbance reduction and fluorescence quenching of EY. New spectrophotometry and fluorescence quenching methods have been developed for the determination of Ag(I) within the ionic association of EY-Ag, with detection limits of 0.17 µM and 2.2 nM, respectively. The fluorescence quenching method offers high sensitivity, good selectivity, high accuracy, and ease of operation, making it suitable for the analysis of silver ions in surface water samples and wastewater samples as well as Ag(I) standard samples. Through the analysis of the absorption spectrum, density functional theory (DFT) theoretical calculations and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, it was found that under weakly acidic conditions, the Ag(I) coordinates with the carboxyl functional group of EY, forming a 1:1 sparingly soluble ionic association that induces static fluorescence quenching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag(I)
Eosin Y
Absorbance
Fluorescence
DFT
NMR

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The interaction between EY and Ag(I) and its effect on fluorescence
The interaction of EY and Ag(I)
Quenching of the fluorescence of EY by Ag(I)
Standard curve and the detection limits

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of EY with carbons numbered.

Absorption spectra of EY, Ag(I) and EY-Ag(I), (a) the concentration of EY is 1.5 × 10−5 M, pH = 4.4, the absorption spectra are recorded by using water as a reference solution. (b) shows the spectra of EY-Ag(I) using the reagent blank as the reference solution and varying the concentration of Ag(I); curves 1 to 5 are 9.3 × 10−7, 1.9 × 10−6, 2.8 × 10−6, 3.8 × 10−6, 4.6 × 10−6 M.

Fluorescence spectra of EY with addition of various concentrations of Ag(I). (a) excitation spectra (400–700 nm) were measured with the emission wavelength at 540 nm and (b) emission spectra (470–650 nm) were measured with excitation wavelength at 515 nm. The EY (1.5 × 10−5 M) was used with pH adjusted to 4.4 by BR buffer. Concentrations of Ag(I) from curve 1 to 6 are 0, 9.3 × 10−8, 1.4 × 10−7, 1.9 × 10−7, 2.4 × 10−7, 2.8 × 10−7 M.

Effect of pH and concentration of EY on fluorescence intensity of the Ag(I)-EY system. (a) Effect of pH, the buffer solution is BR solution, the concentration of EY and Ag(I) were 1.5 × 10−5 M and 1.9 × 10−7 M, respectively. (b) Effect of concentration of EY, pH = 4.4, the concentration of EY was 5.0 × 10−6 M to 3.0 × 10−5 M, and Ag(I) was 1.9 × 10−7 M. (c) Effect of time, the buffer solution is BR solution, the concentration of EY and Ag(I) were the same as (a).
The proposed forms of EY, the complex position of Ag and EY, and the energy difference before and after combination.
The charge and electrostatic potential of EY(I), (II) and EY(III).

Absorption spectra of EY and EY-Ag. The concentration of EY and Ag(I) are 1.5 × 10−5 M and 1.9 × 10−5 M, respectively, pH = 4.4. The absorption spectrum of EY was recorded using water as a reference solution, the absorption spectrum of Ag(I)-EY was measured using Ag(I) solution as the reference solution.

The effects of temperature on fluorescence Ag(I)-EY systems. (a) Effects of temperature on ΔF(F0/F) (n = 3), concentration of EY is 1.5 × 10−5 M, concentration of Ag(I) is 1.9 × 10−7 M. pH4.4. (b) Stem-Volmer plots of Ag(I)-EY system at 293 K, 303 K and 313 K, Concentrations of Ag(I) are 9.3 × 10−8, 1.4 × 10−7, 1.9 × 10−7, 2.4 × 10−7, 2.8 × 10−7 M.
1H and 13C chemical shift of EY and EY-Ag in DMSO‑d 6 .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Bolded values indicate a change on coordination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Values of the quenching constants of Ag(I)-EY systems.
Related parameters of the calibration graphs and the detection limits.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Detection limit calculated as the signal equal to three times the standard deviation of the analysis blank. The calculation formula is LOD = k*Sb/b in which k is the confidence factor and equals to 3.3, Sb is the standard deviation of blank, b is the slope of the regression line.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different methods for Ag(I) detection.

row.entry: Atomic absorption and plasma emission spectrometry
For the major components no interferences were encountered.

chelation-enhanced fluorescence, selective complexation with Hg2+, Ag+ and Ag nanoparticles
Simultaneous detection of trace Ag(I) and Cu(II) ions
good selectivity, high accuracy and operate simply
high sensitivity, good selectivity, high accuracy and operate simply

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Relative error was the ratio of the absolute error of the measurement to the actual measurement. Relative error = (C-CT)/CT, C was the concentration of Ag(I) measured in the presence of other coexisting ions, and CT was 1.9 × 10−7 M.

head.ce:title: A novel determination method for Ag(I) in environmental samples based on reduction of absorbance and fluorescence quenching of Eosin Y

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Primary Education, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, University of Waikato, Hamilton, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, University of Waikato, Hamilton, New Zealand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ag(I) and Eosin Y (EY) can interact directly under weakly acidic conditions to form a sparingly soluble ionic association which results in absorbance reduction and fluorescence quenching of EY. New spectrophotometry and fluorescence quenching methods have been developed for the determination of Ag(I) within the ionic association of EY-Ag, with detection limits of 0.17 µM and 2.2 nM, respectively. The fluorescence quenching method offers high sensitivity, good selectivity, high accuracy, and ease of operation, making it suitable for the analysis of silver ions in surface water samples and wastewater samples as well as Ag(I) standard samples. Through the analysis of the absorption spectrum, density functional theory (DFT) theoretical calculations and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, it was found that under weakly acidic conditions, the Ag(I) coordinates with the carboxyl functional group of EY, forming a 1:1 sparingly soluble ionic association that induces static fluorescence quenching.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its excellent electrical and optical properties, silver is widely used in many industrial fields, such as electronics, photography, imaging, electroplating and ink manufacture [1]. In addition, the antibacterial properties of silver allows it to be used in the medical and pharmaceutical industries and in food preservation [2]. Nowadays, silver nanoparticles are also extensively used in consumer products; although these nanoparticles are relatively stable in freshwater and in association with dissolved organic matter, under saline conditions they rapidly dissolve and release toxic silver ions [3,4].

Silver ions inhibit the microbial respiratory chain directly [5] and are highly toxic to aquatic organisms inducing a variety of cytopathic changes [6]. Silver ions can bioaccumulate in humans and other species to reach toxic levels [5,7,8], and can interact with many physiological metabolites to damage the health of the organism [9–12]. Common effects observed in humans include argyria, gastroenteritis, neuronal disorders, mental fatigue, rheumatism, knotting of cartilage, cytopathological effects in fibroblast, keratinocytes [6,13]. For these reasons, it is desirable to develop a sensitive method for detecting environmental silver ions.

A number of methods have been developed for the detection of Ag(I), encompassing colorimetry [14], fluorescence [15], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [16] and mass spectrometry (MS) [17]. However, some of these approaches entail high-cost and complexity and are not easily deployable in field settings. We present below a straightforward, efficient and selective method for the detection of silver ions, which is well-suited for environmental applications.

Eosin Y (EY) is an acidic dye in which a fluorescein matrix molecule is substituted by four bromine atoms (Fig. 1 ). The electron-withdrawing nature of the bromine atoms causes chromogenic effects. Because of the presence of an extended conjugate system and multiple chromophores, EY has high photoluminescence quantum yields and a high molar absorption coefficient [18]. Therefore, it has been widely used in spectral analysis, photonics research and electrochemistry.

In spectral analysis, EY is generally used as an ion association reagent. However, in past studies, it has not been used for the direct determination of metal ions because the binary chelate between metal ions and EY causes no obvious spectral changes, although a form of ternary ion association may lead to noticeable changes in the spectrum [19,20]. Based on spectral changes [19,21–25], absorption, fluorescence and resonance Rayleigh scattering methods have been established for the determination of some compounds. For example, Wu et al. (2017) have developed a fluorescence quenching method to determinate quizalofop-p-ethyl [20], Attia et al. (2016) reported a spectrofluorimetric determination method of thioridazine and flupentixolin dosage forms [26], and Oue et al. (1988) proposed an extraction-spectrofluorimetric method to quantify silver ions [27]. All these methods are based on the formation of an intermediate binary complex, and subsequently the formation of a ternary complex with EY; however, they are complicated, high-cost and not readily applicable in the field.

In this study, it was found that Ag(I) and EY can directly interact, forming a sparingly soluble ionic association under weakly acidic conditions with a pH range of 4.4–4.6. This interaction leads to absorbance reduction and fluorescence quenching of EY. The extents of reduction in absorbance and quenching in fluorescence intensity were directly proportional to the concentration of Ag(I). Based on this observation, two new methods for the determination of Ag(I) were established using spectrometry and fluorescence quenching. These methods eliminate the need to create intermediate complexes and are extremely simple. This report details the development and application of these methods to environmental samples, as well as characterization of the complex thus formed.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-8500 spectrophotometer (Shanghai Tianmei, China). NMR spectra were acquired using a Jeol ECZR 600 MHz NMR spectrometer.

EY was purchased from Merck. and a stock solution (1.0 × 10−3 M) was prepared. Ag(I) (9.3 × 10-4 M) as aqueous AgNO3 was purchased from the Institute of Standard Samples in the Ministry of Environmental Protection, China. Working solutions were freshly prepared by diluting the corresponding stock solutions.

Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions were used to control the pH of the interaction system, which was adjusted to 4.4–4.6 with the mixed acid (composed of 0.040 M H3PO4, CH3COOH and H3BO3) and 0.20 M NaOH. The pH values were monitored with a pH meter. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

BR buffer solution (1.0 mL), EY solution (1.0 mL, 1.5 × 10−4 M) and the required amount of Ag (I) were added into a 10 mL volumetric flask and made up to volume with water. The solution was mixed and allowed to stand for 5 min before its absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired. The decreased absorbance and fluorescence intensity (A and ΔF) of the system were represented by ΔA = A 0 − A and ΔF = F 0 − F , where A and F, A0 and F 0 were the absorbance and fluorescence intensities of the complex and the reagent blank (no added Ag(I)), respectively.

EY (∼20 mg) and air-dried EY-Ag product were each dissolved in 1 mL of hexadeuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6). The 1H and 13C assignments of EY and EY-Ag were achieved through 1H, 13C spectra and 2D NMR experiments: 1H–1H correlation spectroscopy (COSY), 1H–13C heteronuclear single quantum coherence (HSQC) and 1H–13C multiple bond correlation (HMBC).

The absorption spectra of EY, Ag(I) and their reaction mixture are shown in Fig. 2 . In the 300–600 nm range, Ag(I) exhibits minimal absorption, while EY alone displays its maximum absorption peak at 514 nm. Upon the addition of Ag(I) and the formation of the EY–Ag ion-association, notable changes in the absorption spectra are observed (Fig. 2 a). In comparison to EY alone, two additional absorption peaks appeared at 209 nm and 307 nm, accompanied by a redshift of the maximum absorption wavelength by 3 nm and a significant decrease in the absorbance intensity. The change of absorbance (A) showed a linear relationship with the concentration of Ag(I), as illustrated in Fig. 2 b. The molar absorption coefficient (ε) was determined to be 4.6 × 104 L mol−1cm−1, with a detection limit of 0.17 µM. Therefore, a new spectrophotometric method for detecting Ag(I) can potentially be developed.

Three kinds of buffer solution including BR, HCl-sodium citrate and HCl-CH3COONa were used to test their effects upon fluorescence intensity, and the results showed that ΔF was maximized using BR buffer solution, the optimum pH was in the range from 4.4 to 4.6 (Fig. 4
a).

Experimental results showed that when the concentration of EY was 1.5 × 10−5 M, ΔF reached a maximum (Fig. 4 b). Presumably a large excess of EY is required to ensure that the solubility equilibrium of the sparingly soluble ionic product is forced to completion with maximum quenching after which point any further addition of EY will cause fluorescence to increase.

At room temperature, the reaction of EY and Ag(I) was completed in 5 min and the quenched fluorescence intensity (ΔF) was stable over 12 h (Fig. 4 c). The fast reaction and excellent stability contribute to the potential of this method for analysis of Ag(I) in the field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between EY and Ag(I) and its effect on fluorescence
The interaction of EY and Ag(I)
ce:section.ce:para: The binding ratio between Ag(I) and EY was determined by the equimolar continuous change method and the molar ratio method. The results showed that Ag(I) and EY follow a 1:1 ratio to form an ion-association complex.

EY exists in different forms including divalent anion, monovalent anion, neutral form and cation by a three-stage dissociation in aqueous solutions, which can be represented by the following scheme:
H 3
L +
⇌
H 2
L ⇋
HL -
⇋
L
2 -
where L represents the anionic moiety of EY. Batistela et al. (2011) reported that the pKa1, pKa2 and pKa3 values of EY in the aqueous solution were 2.10, 2.85 and 4.95, respectively [28]. Therefore, at pH 4.4, EY should exist mainly as the monovalent anion (HL−). This was confirmed by the maximum absorption wavelength of EY at pH 4.4, which was observed at 514 nm, Fig. 2 a, consistent with that reported by Zhang et al. (2013) who showed that the absorption maxima of dianion, monoanion, neutral quinonoid and neutral lactone forms of EY were located at the wavelengths of 506, 512, 443, and 288 nm, respectively [29].

However, there are three possibilities for the formation of HL−, ionization of the resonance interconnected hydroxyl/carbonyl groups at C6 or C3 or ionization of the carboxyl group at C-7ʹ. Calculations show four potential structures resulting from interaction of these with Ag(I), Fig. 5 . For three of these (EY(I)-Ag, EY(II)-Ag and EY(III)-Ag the silver ion has bidentate coordination to the O− on C3 or C6 and to a Br atom on a vicinal carbon forming a five-membered ring, whereas for the fourth (EY(III)-Agʹ), the silver ion is monodentate and coordinated to the O− on C-7ʹ.

The coordination mode of Ag(I) and EY was calculated by the density functional method of quantum chemistry (B3LYP) [30,31]. The LanL2DZ pseudopotential basis for the Ag atom, and 6–311 + g* basis for other atoms was used. Considering the effect of the solvent on the system, the polarimetric continuum solvation model (PCM) [32] was adopted to optimize the whole calculation process. All the parameters were allowed to relax, and all the calculations converged to an optimized geometry which corresponds to a true energy minimum revealed by the lack of imaginary values in the wavenumber calculations.

The energy change associated with formation of monodentate EY(III)-Agʹ is most favorable and thus this is the most likely final product. To further confirm this conclusion, we calculated the charge and electrostatic potential of EY(I), (II) and EY(III), as shown in Fig. 6 . The results showed that the negative charge of EY(III) was mainly concentrated in the two oxygen atoms (−0.466 and −0.528, respectively) of the carboxyl, so Ag(I) should combine with one of the two oxygen atoms at this position to form ion-association.

The structure of the product of EY and Ag(I) was further investigated using 1D and 2D NMR experiments. The proposed coordination position of the Ag(I) to the EY was determined by comparing the 13C chemical shift assignment with the EY blank.

The 1H assignments of the EY and EY-Ag were achieved through the 1H–1H COSY experiment, subsequently, the carbon atoms bearing hydrogens were assigned using the HSQC experiment. The HMBC spectrum allowed the assignment of the remaining quaternary carbons. The 1H and 13C chemical shifts of EY and EY-Ag are summarized in Table 1 and the carbon numbering can be found in Fig. 1. The chemical shifts of EY matched with literature reports [33–35]. It was evident that the chemical shifts of C2′ and C9 were most affected by coordination, shifting from 140.769 ppm to 136.975 ppm and 157.504 ppm to 155.311 ppm respectively. This indicates that a coordination between Ag (I) and EY occurred, and the coordination position is at the C7ʹ carboxyl group. This observation aligned with the findings reported by Anselmi et al. (2017) [34], who investigated the coordination between EY and Na+, Al3+ and Pd2+. For C2′, they reported the chemical shift changed from 141.71 ppm (EY-Na) to 131.95 ppm (EY-Al) and 139.47 ppm (EY-Pd); for C9, it moved from 157.87 ppm (EY-Na) to 152.33 ppm (EY-Al) and 154.88 ppm (EY-Pd). The change on C7′ was not as pronounced as reported in the EY–Al and EY-Pd systems. The nature of the metal might contribute to this difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of the fluorescence of EY by Ag(I)
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental results showed that Ag(I) affected the fluorescence quenching of EY significantly. The reason for quenching was not due to collision between the fluorophore (EY molecule) and the quenching agent [Ag(I)], but due to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between Ag(I) and EY. Therefore, this is a static quenching, not a dynamic quenching. In addition to the formation of a 1:1 stable ionic association revealed from experiments and quantum chemistry calculations, the fluorescence quenching property also showed that association had occurred.

The change of the absorption spectrum indicates static quenching as opposed to dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching occurs in the excited state and thus the absorption spectrum of the ground state complex remains unaffected [36]. The decrease of ε value after the formation of the complex results in a decrease of transition photons causing fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence is calculated using Equation
(1)
.
(1)
F = 2.303
×
Y f
×
I 0
× ε × b × c

In which Yf is the quantum yield, I0 is the incident light intensity, b is the liquid cell thickness and c is concentration, which in this experiment are all constant. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity must have decreased with the decrease of the ε value.

Dynamic quenching is caused by energy transfer between the excited fluorophore and the quenching agent. Thus, increasing the temperature might not only reduce the viscosity of the solvent, but also accelerate molecular motion, which is conducive to increased quenching. In contrast, the effect of increased temperature on static quenching is to decrease the stability of the complex, and thus reduce quenching. The experimental results indicated that the quenched fluorescence intensity (ΔF) decreased with the increasing temperature, Fig. 8
a, indicating that static quenching is occurring.

Values of the quenching constants at the temperatures (293 K–313 K) were calculated in Fig. 8 b and Table 2 according to the Stern-Volmer formula, Equation
(2) :
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
D
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
D
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the EY in Ag(I)-free solution and at a given Ag(I) concentration, [D] is the given Ag(I) concentration, Ksv is the fluorescence quenching constant, Kq is the apparent quenching constant, τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime.

The result showed that the Ksv value decreased from 9.0 × 106 L mol−1 to 7.1 × 106 L mol−1 with the increase of temperature. Assuming that the fluorescence lifetime of fluorescent molecule (EY) was 108 s [37], its Kq were in the range from 9.0 × 1014 to 7.1 × 1014 L mol−1 s−1. All these values were much higher than the maximum collision quenching constant 1–2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1 of general quenchers [38], which also indicated that the Ag(I)-EY system was a static quenching system.
In summary, the main cause of fluorescence quenching was the formation of a 1:1 ionic association between Ag(I) and EY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve and the detection limits
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimum experimental conditions, the F and A values were plotted against the concentration of Ag (I). All the parameters of the standard curves and the limits of detection (LOD) are listed in Table 3 . The results showed that the LOD of the fluorescence method was 2.2 nM, that of the spectrophotometry method was 0.17 µM. Thus, the fluorescence method was more sensitive than the spectrophotometry method. The sensitivity of the fluorescence method was also higher than the common spectrophotometry, fluorescence, atomic absorption spectrometry and electrochemical methods in the literature, Table 4 , which make it a viable alternative method for Ag(I) detection at trace level.

To investigate the effect of the presence of other metal ions, 22 metal ions were selected as interference for the determination of Ag (I) and the results are shown in Table 5 . 100–2000 fold relative concentrations of other metal ions did not interfere with the determination. Therefore, the method had good selectivity and could be used for the determination of trace Ag(I) in drinking water or surface water and industrial wastewater.

The concentration of Ag(I) in actual samples was controlled between 9.3 × 10−3 and 0.46 µM by dilution or concentration. According to the procedure described in section 2.2, the concentrations of Ag(I) were determined using the fluorescence quenching method.

According to water quality monitoring and sampling standards (HJ/J 91–2002, China), 8 samples of surface water and wastewater were collected. After filtration, the correct amount of water sample was removed and analyzed using the fluorescence method. The standard method for detecting Ag(I) is flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS), and the results using the fluorescence method were verified by comparison with the standard method, and were consistent, Table 6 . The recovery rates were between 91.38 % and 132.04 %. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the determinations for analyte ions were in the range of 1.82–4.25 %. Therefore, the method had good accuracy and precision.

The method was validated with standard samples (Institute of Standard Samples of the State Environmental Protection Administration, China) and the results are listed in Table 7 . The relative error of the fluorescence method ranged from 4.53 % to 4.84 %, and there was no significant difference (α = 0.05) when compared with the published standard values for both the fluorescence method and the FAAS method. Therefore, the new method was both accurate and repeatable.

In this paper, spectrophotometry and fluorescence quenching methods for the determination of Ag(I) have been established based on the formation of an ionic association between EY and Ag(I). This fluorescence quenching method exhibits high sensitivity, good selectivity, high accuracy and simplicity in operation. It is applicable for analyzing silver ions in surface water samples, wastewater samples and standard samples. This work reveals that the coordination of Ag(I) with Eosin Y occurs at the carboxyl function group, which aligns with the results obtained from theoretical calculations. The 1:1 sparingly soluble ionic association is responsible for fluorescence static quenching.

Jian Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ling Chen: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yanan Li: Writing – review & editing. Wei Shen: Data curation, Investigation. Merilyn Manley-Harris: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Zerbetto, Patterning through controlled submolecular motion: rotaxane-based switches and logic gates that function in solution and polymer films, Angew. Chem., 117 (2005) 3122-3127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Y-type, C-rich DNA probe for electrochemical detection of silver ion and cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Gong, X. Li, Y-type, C-rich DNA probe for electrochemical detection of silver ion and cysteine, Analyst, 136 (2011) 2242-2246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signaling recognition events with fluorescent sensors and switches

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mechanistic study of the antibacterial effect of silver ions on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Its antibacterial properties and mechanism of action

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bactericidal actions of a silver ion solution on Escherichia coli, studied by energy-filtering transmission electron microscopy and proteomic analysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxic effects of silver ions on early developing zebrafish embryos distinguished from silver nanoparticles
